title: Slab-built hanging wall planter
subtitle: Flat-backed slab planter with a curved front pocket for a small pot
excerpt: A wall-mounted planter with a flat slab back panel and a curved D-shaped front pocket formed around a jar mould. The back panel has two pierced holes for hanging on cord or nails. Approximately 150 mm wide, 120 mm tall, and 80 mm deep. No kiln is required.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on slab-building methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness; the back panel of this planter is a slab, and the front pocket is a curved slab draped around a jar mould
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying slip before pressing them together, essential for bonding the pocket to the back panel
A creamy mixture of clay and water used as adhesive at all joins in this piece; dry clay will not bond without it
The drying stage at which clay holds its shape firmly enough to be removed from a mould and joined to another clay piece
The drying stage at which all moisture has left the clay and the piece is ready for painting and sealing
Fully dried but unfired clay that is fragile and will dissolve if wetted before sealing
Shaping a clay slab by pressing or draping it against a curved mould so the clay takes on the mould's profile

content.text: This wall planter consists of a flat
slab
back panel and a curved D-shaped front pocket formed by
drape-forming
around a jar or bottle. The two pieces are joined at the
leather-hard
stage using
score-and-slip
. No kiln is required. Finished dimensions are approximately 150 mm wide, 120 mm tall, and 80 mm deep. A plastic liner pot sits inside the pocket.

materials.name: Cling film (to line the jar mould)

content.text: Preparation
Select a jar or bottle with a rounded profile approximately 80 mm in diameter to use as the pocket mould. Wrap the jar in cling film so the clay does not adhere to it. Prepare
slip

by mixing a walnut-sized piece of clay with water to a thick cream consistency. Knead all working clay for two minutes before rolling. The back panel and the front pocket are built separately and joined at the leather-hard stage, so plan to spread the build across two sittings approximately 12-18 hours apart.
Construction

Make the back panel. Roll a slab 7 mm thick. Cut a rectangle 150 mm wide by 120 mm tall. This is the flat back of the planter that will rest against the wall. Smooth the top surface with a damp sponge. Add incised or stamped decoration to the upper portion of the back panel (the area that will be visible above the pocket) while the slab is still soft.

Pierce two hanging holes. Use the needle tool to pierce two holes in the upper portion of the back panel, each 15 mm from the top edge and 40 mm in from the side edges. Widen each hole to at least 5 mm diameter by rotating the needle tool. These holes will hold the hanging cord.
Allow the back panel to reach the leather-hard stage (12-18 hours) on a flat board before proceeding. A flat-drying back panel is important for the planter to hang flush against the wall.

Make the front pocket. Roll a fresh slab 6 mm thick and cut a rectangle approximately 150 mm wide by 160 mm tall. Lay this over the cling-filmed jar so that the long dimension wraps around the front curve of the jar and the excess at the top and bottom overhangs. The slab should wrap around approximately half the jar circumference (the D-shape). Press the slab gently against the jar surface.

Trim the pocket shape. While the slab is draped on the jar, use the fettling knife to trim the two straight side edges so they are flush and straight (these will become the join edges where the pocket meets the back panel). Trim the bottom edge to a gentle curve. Allow the pocket to firm to the leather-hard stage on the jar mould.

Remove the pocket from the mould. At the leather-hard stage the pocket will hold its D-shape when lifted away from the jar. Slide it off and peel away the cling film. The pocket's two straight edges are the join surfaces.

Join pocket to back panel. Score the two straight side edges of the pocket and the corresponding area on the front face of the back panel where the pocket will sit. Apply slip to all scored surfaces and press the pocket firmly against the back panel. The bottom of the pocket sits approximately 10 mm above the bottom edge of the back panel. Hold the join for 60 seconds. Support the pocket at the correct angle with a balled-up cloth if needed while the slip sets.

Add a base strip. Cut a small slab strip 15 mm wide and long enough to span across the bottom opening of the pocket. Score and slip it into place across the bottom of the pocket opening to close the base. This prevents the liner pot from falling through.

Reinforce all joins. Run a thin coil of soft clay along each interior join line where the pocket meets the back panel. Press firmly and smooth. Leave the planter propped against a support while the joins dry.
Drying
Prop the assembled planter at a slight forward angle so the pocket faces up; this prevents the wet joins from pulling open under gravity. Allow 48-3 days at room temperature to reach the
bone-dry
stage. The
greenware
piece is most fragile at the pocket-to-back joins. Do not apply lateral pressure to the pocket while drying.
Finishing

Paint with acrylic paint and allow to dry fully. Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water to all exterior surfaces and the interior of the pocket. Thread hanging cord through the two pierced holes and tie a knot on each end behind the panel to secure.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. This planter is not suitable as a water vessel. Use a plastic liner pot inside the pocket so water from plant care does not contact the clay directly. Keep the planter indoors. Do not hang in a bathroom or other high-humidity environment. Wipe with a dry cloth.
Variations
Arch-top back panel: cut the top of the back panel in an arch shape before it dries to give the planter a more decorative silhouette.
Double-pocket planter: cut a back panel 300 mm tall and attach two pockets at different heights for two plants, staggered left and right.
Incised botanical panel: at the leather-hard stage, cover the visible back panel with an incised design. Use leaves, ferns, or geometric lines. Do this before joining the pocket.

rows.problem: Pocket pulls away from the back panel during drying
rows.cause: The leather-hard pocket and back panel were at different moisture levels, causing differential shrinkage
rows.fix: At leather-hard, re-apply slip to the join, press closed, and reinforce with an interior coil fillet. Slow the drying by covering lightly with a damp cloth

rows.problem: Back panel bows outward during drying
rows.cause: The panel dried on an uneven surface or faster on one face than the other
rows.fix: At leather-hard, press flat on the board and weight lightly with another board. Allow to dry in the same position

rows.problem: Hanging holes crack at their edges
rows.cause: Holes were pierced too close to the edge of the panel
rows.fix: For bone-dry cracks, fill with undiluted PVA and allow to cure. Reinforce by threading the cord twice through each hole rather than relying on a single thread

rows.problem: Pocket opening is too narrow for the liner pot
rows.cause: The jar mould was too small in diameter
rows.fix: At leather-hard, use a finger or round object to gently widen the pocket opening. If too dry to reshape, use a smaller liner pot
